skill.name: red-team-tactics
skill.description: Use MITRE ATT&CK concepts to plan authorized red team exercises, discuss attack phases, review detection gaps, and structure reports.
skill.summary: Plan authorized red team exercises and reporting with MITRE ATT&CK concepts.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static command-execution and weak-cryptography alerts are false positives caused by Markdown formatting and ordinary words in SKILL.md. However, the skill contains dual-use red team guidance about initial access, credential access, defense evasion, lateral movement, C2, exfiltration, and Active Directory attacks, so publication requires human review and strong marketplace safeguards.

high_findings.title: Dual-Use Offensive Red Team Methodology
high_findings.description: The skill provides structured red team methodology covering phishing, public exploits, valid credentials, defense evasion, lateral movement, and Active Directory attack categories. This can support authorized assessments, but it can also help misuse if published without review.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections directly list offensive tactics and objectives. Confidence is high because the content is explicit, although it does not include executable code or commands.

medium_findings.title: C2 And Exfiltration Concepts Present
medium_findings.description: The lifecycle diagram and phase table include C2 and exfiltration as red team phases. This confirms the static C2 keyword finding as dual-use conceptual content, not as evidence of malware implementation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The terms C2 and exfiltration appear in clear attack-lifecycle context. Confidence is high for dual-use risk, but there is no code, endpoint, implant, or data-transfer instruction.

medium_findings.title: Credential Access And Discovery Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill describes credential access, discovery, internal network mapping, and valid credentials as red team concepts. This content is sensitive but presented as high-level methodology rather than step-by-step exploitation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly discuss credential and discovery phases. Confidence is high enough for a medium finding because the content is conceptual and lacks operational procedures.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fence Misread As External Command Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution at SKILL.md line 17. The evidence is a Markdown code fence around an attack lifecycle diagram, not executable shell or Ruby code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The backticks are visible Markdown delimiters and contain only a diagram. No command interpreter, script, or user-controlled command input is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts at SKILL.md lines 3 and 181 do not identify cryptographic code. The cited text is the skill description and an ethical boundary about not destroying production data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No cryptographic algorithm, cipher choice, hash function, or implementation appears at the cited lines. The finding is consistent with substring matching rather than semantic evidence.

content.value_statement: Security teams need a clear way to frame red team work without losing scope or reporting quality. This skill organizes authorized exercise planning, attack-phase review, detection gaps, and final reports.
actual_capabilities: Maps red team activity to MITRE ATT&CK lifecycle phases.
Compares passive and active reconnaissance trade-offs.
Summarizes initial access, privilege escalation, lateral movement, and collection concepts.
Frames defense evasion and operational security considerations for authorized exercises.
Guides report structure for attack narratives and detection gaps.
Defines ethical boundaries for scoped red team work.
limitations: Does not provide exploit code, payloads, commands, or automation.
Does not verify legal authorization or scope documents.
Does not replace a formal rules-of-engagement review.
Can be misused if applied outside authorized security work.

use_cases.title: Scope A Red Team Exercise
use_cases.description: Create a phase-based plan that keeps objectives, access paths, and reporting expectations aligned with the approved scope.

use_cases.title: Review Detection Coverage
use_cases.description: Use the technique categories to discuss where monitoring, alerting, and response controls should detect activity.

use_cases.title: Prepare Executive Findings
use_cases.description: Turn a technical attack chain into a clear narrative with objectives, detection gaps, and recommended improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Explain The Red Team Lifecycle
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the authorized red team lifecycle using MITRE ATT&CK phases. Keep the explanation high level and include ethical boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Plan A Scoped Exercise
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me outline an authorized red team exercise for this approved scope: [scope]. Include phases, objectives, guardrails, and reporting needs.

prompt_templates.title: Map Detection Gaps
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this authorized exercise narrative: [narrative]. Identify likely detection gaps and suggest defensive improvements without exploit instructions.

prompt_templates.title: Build A Final Report Narrative
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert these authorized red team notes into a professional report narrative: [notes]. Include objectives, attack path summary, detection failures, and remediation themes.

output_examples.input: Summarize an authorized exercise plan for testing exposed services.
output_examples.output: The response would outline approved objectives, reconnaissance boundaries, initial access assumptions, detection expectations, stop conditions, and reporting sections.

output_examples.input: Turn red team notes into detection improvement themes.
output: Identity monitoring should cover unusual credential use and privileged account activity.
Endpoint logging should highlight suspicious administrative tool use.
Network detections should focus on unexpected internal movement and data staging.

output_examples.input: Create an executive summary from an attack narrative.
output_examples.output: The response would describe the business impact, the validated control gaps, the affected security domains, and prioritized remediation themes.

best_practices: Use the skill only for authorized, documented security work.
Pair every attack phase with detection, containment, and reporting expectations.
Keep outputs focused on planning, analysis, and remediation instead of operational misuse.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill to plan activity outside approved scope.
Do not request exploit instructions, payloads, persistence steps, or data theft workflows.
Do not treat the skill as legal approval for a red team engagement.

faq.question: Is this skill for offensive security work?
faq.answer: Yes. It is intended for authorized red team planning, detection review, and reporting.

faq.question: Does it include exploit code or commands?
faq.answer: No. The source content is high-level methodology and does not include exploit code, payloads, or command sequences.

faq.question: Can defenders use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. Defenders can use it to map likely attack phases to detection gaps and control improvements.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level high?
faq.answer: The skill discusses sensitive red team concepts, including defense evasion, credential access, C2, exfiltration, and lateral movement.

faq.question: Does the skill run external tools?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed file only declares read-oriented tools and contains Markdown guidance.

faq.question: What should users prepare before using it?
faq.answer: Users should prepare written authorization, a defined scope, rules of engagement, and reporting requirements.
